bibliographic.citation: John Small, ed., English Metrical Homilies from Manuscripts of the Fourteenth Century, with an Introduction and Notes (Edinburgh: William Paterson, 1862).
bibliographic.title: English Metrical Homilies from Manuscripts of the Fourteenth Century, with an Introduction and Notes

entities.description: Small's edition of the opening fragment of the Northern Homily Cycle, with an introduction, a three-snippet manuscript plate, the verse text through the Purification, and textual notes. The continuous copy text is principally Edinburgh, Royal College of Physicians, MS of Cursor Mundi, but substantial losses are supplied from Gg.5.31 and Ashmole 42.
entities.label: John Small, English Metrical Homilies (1862)
notes: CME00029 encodes the title-page content and printed pp. 1-171 only.

The volume calls itself printed from the Edinburgh manuscript, but its notes identify several continuous supplied runs from the Cambridge and Ashmole witnesses; the edition is therefore not modeled as a homogeneous transcription of one codex.

rights.basis: The publication appeared in 1862, and the U-M item applies No Copyright-United States to the encoded public-domain edition materials.
rights.component: Underlying 1862 edition text, typography, apparatus, and printed plate
notes: This does not waive separate contractual or provider terms governing modern scan files.

access.provider: Royal College of Physicians of Edinburgh

entities.description: An early-fourteenth-century vellum miscellany of fifty leaves in three textual fragments and hands. Its Northern Homily Cycle portion, ff. 16r-36v, preserves the prologue, Ratio, and first thirteen homilies and breaks near the beginning of the second Purification exemplum. Small used it as his principal witness.

holding.notes: No reliable current numbered shelfmark or item-level online catalog record was verified. The Northern Homily Cycle is at ff. 16r-36v; Small's printed plate also samples fols. 1 and 37 from adjacent Cursor Mundi fragments.

entities.label: Edinburgh, Royal College of Physicians, MS of Cursor Mundi
notes: The North Riding localization applies to the Northern Homily Cycle copying hand, not automatically to the work's composition or the manuscript's later Edinburgh location.

physicalDescription.extent: 50 leaves; the Northern Homily Cycle occupies ff. 16r-36v, with three leaves missing after fol. 24
physicalDescription.format: Thin quarto miscellany; text in two columns, normally 43 lines, with three fragments or hands; leaves later mounted in cardboard frames and bound
notes: The Northern Homily Cycle section is in one textura hand with Latin Gospel rubrics in red.
The manuscript was bequeathed to the College in 1741 by Dr John Drummond.

reviewNotes: The edited selection, complete 1862 publication, U-M encoding, repository XML, three physical manuscripts, complete print scan, and partial manuscript plate are distinct entities.

Small's introduction and notes control the witness dependencies. His principal Edinburgh manuscript has no verified current shelfmark beyond the scholarly designation MS of Cursor Mundi; no number is invented.

The cached print PDF is complete relative to the 1862 volume. CME00029 itself maps discontiguously to the title page and printed pp. 1-171, omitting the intervening plate and preliminaries and the notes on pp. 173-188.
Whole-object manuscript access checks include physical-object surfaces and adjacent works. A selected public plate or text-bearing folio range is not treated as a complete codex surrogate.

lineage.summary: CME00029 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan electronic selection from John Small's 1862 English Metrical Homilies. The encoding retains the edition title page and the verse text on printed pp. 1-171: a prologue, the Ratio, and the first thirteen Northern Homily Cycle homilies from Advent through the Purification. Small's principal source was the Northern Homily Cycle portion of the Royal College of Physicians of Edinburgh manuscript known as the MS of Cursor Mundi, with explicitly delimited supplies and readings from Cambridge University Library MS Gg.5.31 and Oxford, Bodleian Library MS Ashmole 42. A complete print scan and derivatives, plus Anne B. Thompson's complete 2008 scholarly edition, are cached. No complete public digital object was found for any of the three physical witnesses; Small's three-snippet printed plate is only a partial facsimile of the Edinburgh codex.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Northern Homily Cycle poet
catalogSummary.date: Underlying cycle probably composed c. 1295-1306; edited selection published 1862
catalogSummary.region: Northern England, perhaps Yorkshire; witness copying and Edinburgh publication are recorded separately
catalogSummary.title: English Metrical Homilies

catalogingSubject.label: English Metrical Homilies
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection encoded from John Small's 1862 English Metrical Homilies: its title-page content and complete printed verse text on pp. 1-171, comprising the Prologue, Ratio, and the first thirteen Northern Homily Cycle homilies from Advent through the Purification. The selection excludes Small's manuscript facsimile plate, introduction and manuscript description, blank p. 172, notes on pp. 173-188, and remaining scan matter. It is neither the complete Northern Homily Cycle, the complete 1862 publication, one physical manuscript, the U-M encoding, nor the checked-in XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Prologue
contentParts.summary: Introduces the cycle's pastoral purpose, audience, and use of Gospel teaching in English verse.

contentParts.label: Ratio
contentParts.summary: A compact statement of the work's rationale and method before the first liturgical homily.

contentParts.label: The First Sunday of Advent
contentParts.summary: Gospel paraphrase, exposition, pastoral teaching, and exemplary narrative for the first Sunday of Advent.

contentParts.label: The Second Sunday of Advent
contentParts.summary: Gospel paraphrase and homiletic exposition for the second Sunday of Advent, including a separately encoded Latin verse passage.

contentParts.label: The Third Sunday of Advent
contentParts.summary: Gospel paraphrase, exposition, pastoral application, and exemplary material for the third Sunday of Advent.

contentParts.label: The Fourth Sunday of Advent
contentParts.summary: Gospel paraphrase, exposition, pastoral application, and exemplary material for the fourth Sunday of Advent.

contentParts.label: The Nativity of Our Lord
contentParts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase and homily for Christmas, with doctrinal exposition and exemplary narrative.

contentParts.label: The Sunday within the Octave of the Nativity
notes: Small's explicitly sourced Cambridge and Ashmole supplies fall within this printed-page range; exact spans are recorded in lineage rather than homogenized here.
contentParts.summary: An extended verse Gospel paraphrase and homily for the Sunday within the Christmas octave.

contentParts.label: The Epiphany
contentParts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase, exposition, pastoral application, and exemplary material for the Epiphany.

contentParts.label: The Sunday within the Octave of the Epiphany
contentParts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase and homiletic teaching for the Sunday within the Epiphany octave.

contentParts.label: The First Sunday after the Octave of the Epiphany
contentParts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase and homily for the first Sunday after the Epiphany octave.

contentParts.label: The Second Sunday after the Octave of the Epiphany
contentParts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase and homily for the second Sunday after the Epiphany octave.

contentParts.label: The Third Sunday after the Octave of the Epiphany
contentParts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase and homily for the third Sunday after the Epiphany octave.

contentParts.label: The Fourth Sunday after the Octave of the Epiphany
contentParts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase and homily for the fourth Sunday after the Epiphany octave.

contentParts.label: The Purification of the Virgin Mary
notes: The printed and encoded endpoint follows the surviving fragment; the complete Northern Homily Cycle continues beyond this selection. Small's final passage from p. 167 line 22 is supplied from Gg.5.31.
contentParts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase, exposition, and exempla for the Purification; the selected fragment ends during the cycle's Purification material.

assertion.notes: A reasoned modern window rather than a dated authorial document; the cycle's date and author remain unknown.

scope.description: Underlying Northern Homily Cycle material represented by the selected opening

assertion.notes: A witness-production date, not an automatic composition date.

scope.description: Earliest surviving witness of the selected opening in the RCPE manuscript
Small's edited selection as cataloged here

extent.description: The encoded medieval content has fifteen cataloged items: a 128-line Prologue, a 40-line Ratio, and thirteen homilies totaling 3,948 further lines. The seventeen XML LG elements include a separately nested thirty-three-line Latin passage in the Second Sunday of Advent homily. The homily count remains thirteen; the manifest item total also counts Prologue and Ratio. Title-page paratext is excluded from these item and line totals.

formStatements.description: The medieval selection is verse, predominantly rhyming octosyllabic couplets; editorial title-page prose is paratext rather than a prose content item.

genres.label: Gospel paraphrase
genres.scheme: METS edition categories and General Corpus local genre

scope.description: Narrative examples embedded across the homily selection
Saints' legends and miracles used as exemplary narratives across the selection
Prologue, Ratio, Gospel paraphrases, homilies, and exempla
Embedded Gospel rubrics, quotations, and an encoded thirty-three-line Latin verse passage within the Second Sunday of Advent homily
English-language edition title page and modern editorial framing retained by the encoding, not the primary language of the medieval text

notes: The preferred title describes Small's 1862 edited selection rather than a medieval witness title or the complete Northern Homily Cycle.
John Small remains edition-level editor, selector, transcriber, and collator; the anonymous medieval poet remains work-level author.
The thirteen homilies are stable content parts, with Prologue and Ratio separately modeled and internal Gospel-paraphrase, exposition, and exemplum boundaries summarized rather than over-segmented.
The RCPE codex, its Northern Homily Cycle hand and folios, Small's three-snippet plate, and the complete print scan are distinct physical, textual, editorial, and digital layers.

The record remains partial because all three complete manuscript objects, exact supplementary-witness loci, the RCPE current shelfmark, fuller competing authorship and dating scholarship, and exact U-M production history remain unresolved.

next_steps: Use the complete-object request routes and shot-list boundaries recorded in lineage.

Map Small's print ranges and brackets only after direct image comparison.

next_steps: Ask RCPE Library and Archive to identify the object from Small's edition, Drummond's 1741 bequest, and the Northern Homily Cycle locus ff. 16r-36v.
Add only a repository-confirmed shelfmark and preserve historical designations separately.
openQuestions.question: What current RCPE shelfmark and catalog description should supplement the scholarly designation MS of Cursor Mundi?

next_steps: Review the underlying Carver, Heffernan, and linguistic arguments rather than relying only on Thompson's synthesis.
Record materially competing claims with attribution and confidence.
Keep anonymous authorship, c. 1295-1306, Yorkshire, Augustinian, and lay oral-delivery statements qualified until that review.
openQuestions.question: How should competing modern arguments about the cycle's exact composition date, Yorkshire origin, Augustinian context, author, and intended performance setting be represented after fuller review?

next_steps: Retrieve and compare the exact current U-M XML bitstream.
Document keying, proofreading, structural revision, and publication dates from U-M records.
Keep the 2016 date scoped to repository review.
openQuestions.question: When and by whom was CME00029 first keyed, and how does the checked-in XML differ from U-M's current source XML?

assertion.notes: A broad work-level localization, not a statement about where the Edinburgh codex was copied or later held.
Modern scholarship suggests Yorkshire as a possibility, while LAEME more securely localizes the RCPE copying hand to the North Riding; those claims are not conflated.
Small's discussion of Richard Rolle and other possible origins is historically important but superseded and inconclusive; it does not justify a named-author record.

scope.description: A recurrent subject in selected passages across the homily sequence
A recurrent subject in selected passages across the homily sequence
Saints, miracles, and exemplary narratives embedded across the homily sequence

summaries.value: An edited opening of the anonymous Northern Homily Cycle, a Middle English verse-sermon sequence arranged for the liturgical year. After a Prologue and Ratio, each selected item begins from a Latin Gospel rubric and develops a Middle English Gospel paraphrase, exposition, pastoral application, and often one or more exempla. The selection proceeds from the first Sunday of Advent through the Purification, addressing repentance, confession, mercy, wealth and poverty, clerical conduct, saints, and miracles.

Prologue; Ratio; homilies for the first through fourth Sundays of Advent; the Nativity; the Sunday within the octave of the Nativity; the Epiphany; the Sunday within its octave; the first through fourth Sundays after its octave; and the Purification of the Virgin Mary.

The cataloging unit is exactly Small's title-page-plus-pp. 1-171 selection as encoded by U-M. The complete 1862 book, Northern Homily Cycle, three manuscripts, digital encoding, repository artifact, scan, and manuscript plate remain separate descriptive and lineage layers.

The U-M page's bibliographic Author label is not propagated as medieval authorship: the title page names John Small as editor, and current scholarship leaves the cycle anonymous. Small's primary Edinburgh witness and supplementary Cambridge and Ashmole witnesses are attributed only at the exact source scopes supported by his notes.

assertion.notes: This is the edition-level selection title, not a medieval title attested in the three witnesses and not evidence that John Small authored the homilies.

titles.value: English Metrical Homilies from Manuscripts of the Fourteenth Century, with an Introduction and Notes

work.summary: An edited opening of the anonymous Northern Homily Cycle, a Middle English verse-sermon sequence arranged for the liturgical year. After a Prologue and Ratio, each selected item begins from a Latin Gospel rubric and develops a Middle English Gospel paraphrase, exposition, pastoral application, and often one or more exempla. The selection proceeds from the first Sunday of Advent through the Purification, addressing repentance, confession, mercy, wealth and poverty, clerical conduct, saints, and miracles.
